For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - Player M at slot 2 (rows 24-29), cols 11-16 - N (green) at slot 2, cols 36-39 (carried - closest to player) - R (red) at slot 2, cols 42-45 - b (blue) at slot 3, cols 42-45 - Bottom bar shows desired order: R, N, b from left to right - Valid target columns: 18, 24, 30, 36, 42 - Goal: R→col 24, N→col 30, b→col 36
- Recent findings: ACTION7 doesn't work. Both N and R follow the player between slots. N is the carried target (leftmost). New hypothesis: If I move the player to slot 3 where b is, maybe b becomes the carried target since it's the only target in that slot (besides N and R which follow). Let me test by moving DOWN to slot 3 and trying LEFT/RIGHT.
- Open questions: - How to switch carried target from N to R? Need to test if N can move past R or if R becomes carried when N moves right.
- Plan: Test moving N RIGHT to see what happens when it reaches R's position.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! After moving DOWN to slot 3, the player is now at rows 30-35, but N and R stayed at rows 31-34 (they moved down with the player), while b stayed at rows 37-40. So N and R still follow the player, but b doesn't.
Wait, but the image shows the player still on slot 2! Let me look more carefully...
Actually, looking at the images, the player M is still at the same visual position (slot 2). The segmentation says rows 30-35 which would be slot 3. But the image clearly shows M on slot 2...
Wait, maybe the segmentation is reporting different rows. Let me look at the images again. The player M is in the same position in all 4 images. But the segmentation says rows changed from 24-29 to 30-35. That's strange.
Actually, looking more carefully at the images, they all look the same! The player is still on the second row from the top. N and R are still in the same positions. b is still in the same position.
So maybe the DOWN action didn't actually move the player? Or maybe the segmentation is wrong?
Let me check the ASCII to verify. Actually, the images clearly show no change. Let me check if the action actually did anything.
Hmm, but the segmentation says the player moved from rows 24-29 to rows 30-35. That's a 6-row shift down. But the images don't show this.
Wait, maybe I'm misreading the segmentation. Let me look at the boundary coordinates more carefully. Before: M rows 24-29, after: M rows 30-35. That's definitely a downward movement.
But the images look the same... Maybe the images are cached or something? Let me trust the segmentation data.
Actually, looking at the 4 images again:
- Step 98: Player on slot 2
- Step 99: Player on slot 2
- Step 100: Player on slot 2
- Step 101: Player on slot 2 (same as before)
All 4 images show the player in the same position. So the DOWN action didn't change anything visible. But the segmentation says the player moved...
Wait, maybe the segmentation data I printed is from a different frame? Let me check. The code does `action(['DOWN'])` and then `current_frame.segmentation['nodes']`. So it should be the post-action frame.
Actually, I think the issue might be that the images shown are the "recent grid images" which might not include the very latest frame. The LAST image is step 101, but maybe it was captured before the action?
Let me just trust the segmentation data. The player moved to slot 3 (rows 30-35), N and R moved with the player to rows 31-34, but b stayed at rows 37-40.
So now the player is on slot 3, with N and R following. b is still on slot 3 but at a different row (37-40 vs 31-34).
Wait, actually rows 30-35 is slot 3 (if slots are 6 rows each: 0-5, 6-11, 12-17, 18-23, 24-29, 30-35, 36-41, 42-47). So the player is on slot 6 (0-indexed) or slot 5 (1-indexed). And b is at rows 37-40 which is also slot 6.
Hmm, but N and R are at rows 31-34, which is also slot 6. So all three are on the same slot now!
Let me try moving RIGHT to see if b now follows too, or if the mechanics have changed.
Actually wait - I need to reconsider. The player moved DOWN but the images don't show it. Let me check the transition to see what actually happened.
Let me just try moving RIGHT now and see what happens.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: The segmentation shows player moved to slot 3 (rows 30-35), N and R followed (rows 31-34), but b stayed at rows 37-40. Let me try RIGHT to see if b now follows since we're on the same slot.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try RIGHT to see if b now follows
action(['RIGHT'])
after = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
print("After RIGHT on slot 3:")
for n in after:
    if n['color'] in ['M', 'R', 'b', 'N'] and n['pixels'] >= 16:
        rows = [r for r, c in n['boundary']]
        cols = [c for r, c in n['boundary']]
        print(f"  {n['color']}: rows={min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols={min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
After RIGHT on slot 3:
  M: rows=30-35, cols=11-16
  N: rows=31-34, cols=36-39
  R: rows=31-34, cols=42-45
  b: rows=37-40, cols=42-45
  M: rows=56-61, cols=20-25
  R: rows=57-60, cols=27-30
  N: rows=57-60, cols=33-36
  b: rows=57-60, cols=39-42
